Make the guajillo chile sauce. Heat water in a kettle or pan. Snip tops off chiles and remove seeds. Toast guajillo chiles over hot pan for 10-15 seconds, turning constantly. Move to bowl and pour hot water over them. Soak 10 minutes until soft. Add chiles to blender with garlic and chicken broth. Blend until smooth. Strain through metal strainer to remove seeds and skin. Taste and add salt if needed. Transfer to airtight container; stores in fridge for weeks. 2. Sauté the chorizo & potato. Heat cast-iron pan over medium heat. Add chorizo to render fat, about 2-3 minutes. Dice potato and add to chorizo. Sauté together 8 minutes until potatoes brown and soften. Note: parboil potatoes after dicing for faster cooking. 3. Soak & sear the bread. Spoon guajillo sauce onto plate and dip bread roll to cover both halves. Add oil drizzle to pan and heat over high heat. Add dipped bread halves and sear both sides. The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.

componentResults.reason: The three recipes collectively require many non-staple ingredients not provided by the user (gochujang, sesame oil, hummus, feta, pickled beets, za'atar, sumac, garam masala, pomegranate seeds, etc.). The best match (Gochujang Steak Bowl) still needs 4-5 specialty/fresh items. One recipe (Za'atar Chicken) doesn't even use the user's beef or egg. While the explanation recommends the Gochujang bowl as best match, the overall recipe set scores poorly on ingredient alignment.

Evaluate this meal suggestion for {{party_size}} people with ingredients {{input}}.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, then give an overall score (the minimum of the three):
(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated ingredients without requiring unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different protein treatment, cuisine, or technique — not two pan-fried chicken dishes)?
Return the overall score.

The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.
Score 1-5:
5 = All ingredients in recipes are either user-provided, pantry staples, or minor acceptable extras (1-2 per recipe).
3 = Several unlisted non-staple ingredients appear but don't dominate.
1 = Recipes are loaded with ingredients the user didn't mention (e.g., needing butter, cream, specialty produce, fresh herbs not named).
Return the score.

The overall score is the minimum of the three criteria.

Prep the noodles and sauce: Soak noodles to rehydrate per package directions. Mix all sauce ingredients in a container until well combined. 2. Stir fry the eggs and vegetables: Heat wok or large pan over high heat with oil. Add beaten egg, swirl around pan until fluffy with browning, chop and set aside. Add more oil, garlic, and white pepper; bloom for 30-45 seconds. Add chopped broccolini, stir fry 1-2 minutes, add a drizzle of sauce, then remove to bowl with eggs. 3. Brown the chicken: Add oil to wok, add chicken slices, brown 2 minutes per side, add sauce for color and seasoning. 4. Bring it all together: Add broccolini and egg back to wok with drained noodles. Add enough sauce to coat, stir fry 5-7 minutes until noodles soften and absorb sauce. Soak the noodles in hot water according to package directions. Stir tamarind paste, fish sauce, palm sugar, and soy sauce until sugar dissolves.\n2. Set wok over high heat with oil. Sear protein until browned and cooked through. Remove and set aside. Add oil, crack in eggs, scramble until cooked, set aside with protein. Wipe wok.\n3. Drain noodles. Add oil to wok, add dried shrimp and bloom 30 seconds until fragrant. Add garlic and ginger, stir fry 15-30 seconds. Add noodles, reserved egg and protein, and sauce. Toss until coated.\n4. Add scallions and bamboo shoots in final minute. Taste and adjust with fish sauce, sugar, or lime. Serve with peanuts, lime wedges, and green onions.","alternatives":[]},{"id":"r074","name":"Thai Chile Pork & Cabbage Stir Fry","name_zh":"泰式辣椒猪肉炒卷心菜","cuisine":"Thai","cooking_method":"stir-fry","effort_level":"medium","flavor_tags":["aromatic","spicy","umami"],"serves":2,"pcsv_roles":{"protein":["ground pork","eggs"],"veggie":["cabbage","thai chilies","shallots"],"sauce":["salt","cooking oil","fish sauce","msg","sesame oil","black sesame seeds"]},"ingredients_have":[],"ingredients_need":["ground pork","cabbage","salt","thai chilies","shallots","cooking oil","fish sauce","msg","sesame oil","eggs","black sesame seeds"],"ingredients":[{"name":"ground pork","amount":"225 g","pcsv":["protein"]},{"name":"cabbage","amount":"1/4, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"salt","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"thai chilies","amount":"3-8, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"shallots","amount":"1, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"cooking oil","amount":"as needed","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"fish sauce","amount":"a spoonful","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"msg","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"sesame oil","amount":"a drizzle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"eggs","amount":"1, fried","pcsv":["protein"]},{"name":"black sesame seeds","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]}],"instructions":"1. Brown the pork: Preheat a pan over medium-high heat. Add the ground pork and press it into the hot pan, making a thin even layer across the hot surface. Allow the pork to sear undisturbed until the bottom begins to brown and take on some color. Using a potato masher, fork, or spatula, break up the pork into small pebble-sized bits. 2. Add the cabbage and steam: Once the pork is mostly cooked through, add in the sliced cabbage. Stir the cabbage into the meat to ensure nothing is burning on the bottom of the pan. Cover to steam and soften the cabbage for a minute or two. Remove the cover. Sauté for another minute or two to get more browning on the meat and edges of the cabbage and evaporate any excess moisture. 3. Finish with aromatics and sauces: Push the cabbage and pork mixture to one side of the pan. Add a drizzle of oil to the open space and add in the shallot and Thai chilies to bloom for about 30 seconds before mixing everything together. Add the fish sauce to deglaze any browning from the bottom of the pan. Turn off the heat. Add a drizzle of sesame oil and a sprinkle of MSG. Taste and adjust as needed. 4. Plate and serve: Serve with a fried egg and a sprinkle of sesame seeds for garnish.","alternatives":[]}]}}},{"event_type":"explanation","data":{"event_type":"explanation","text":"You’re set up for a Reduce heat to lowest setting and simmer covered for 20 minutes until water absorbs and rice steams. Taste and add salt as needed.\n\nStep 2: Season & sear the chicken. Butterfly or pound chicken breasts to 3/4\" (2cm) thickness. Sprinkle both sides with salt. Combine equal parts za'atar, garam masala, sumac, and cumin. Liberally coat both sides of chicken. Preheat pan over high heat with oil. Sear chicken to create dark crust without burning, flip to finish cooking. Ensure internal temperature reaches 155°F/68°C. Rest for a few minutes before dicing.\n\nStep 3: Assemble the bowl. Stir once or twice, then reduce heat to low. Cover with lid and set timer for 10 minutes. 3. After 10 minutes, stir rice once or twice, cover, and set timer for another 10 minutes. 4. Test rice doneness. Add remaining cooking liquid as needed to reach desired consistency. Add more liquid for loose texture; use less or continue reducing for compact, sticky texture. 5. Pour in cornstarch gel and blend to create stable emulsion. Taste and adjust salt. Store in fridge up to 3-4 days. 3.
